title: JavaScript Essentials
description: The language of the web. Variables, functions, async, DOM — the must-knows.

questions.q: Which keyword creates a variable whose value CAN be reassigned later?
questions.explanation: let allows reassignment. const does not. Always prefer const, fall back to let only when reassignment is genuinely needed.

questions.q: What happens if you reassign a const variable?
options: A TypeError is thrown at runtime
questions.explanation: TypeError: Assignment to constant variable. const bindings are immutable; the variable cannot point to a new value.

questions.q: What is the scope of a variable declared with 'let' inside a {} block?
options: Block-scoped — only accessible inside that block
questions.explanation: let and const are block-scoped. var is function-scoped, which is why var is generally avoided in modern code.

questions.q: What does this code log? `const arr = [1, 2]; arr.push(3); console.log(arr);`
questions.explanation: const prevents reassigning the variable, but the array itself is mutable. You can push, pop, splice — just not reassign arr.

questions.q: Which declaration is HOISTED but throws ReferenceError if accessed before its declaration line?
questions.explanation: let/const are hoisted but uninitialized until their line. Accessing them before is the 'temporal dead zone' error.

questions.q: Which is NOT a primitive type in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: The 7 primitives: string, number, boolean, null, undefined, symbol, bigint. Arrays and objects are reference types.

questions.q: What does `typeof null` return?
questions.explanation: A famous JavaScript bug preserved for backwards compatibility. typeof null === 'object' even though null is a primitive.

questions.q: Which value is FALSY in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: The falsy values are: false, 0, '', null, undefined, NaN, 0n. Note: '0' (string), [] and {} are all truthy!

questions.q: What's the difference between `null` and `undefined`?
options: null is an intentional 'no value'; undefined means a value was never assigned
null is a number, undefined is an object
null is for objects, undefined is for arrays
questions.explanation: Use null when you mean 'deliberately empty'. undefined is JavaScript's default for unassigned variables and missing properties.

questions.q: What is the result of `0.1 + 0.2 === 0.3`?
questions.explanation: Classic IEEE 754 issue: 0.1 + 0.2 === 0.30000000000000004. Use Number.EPSILON or a decimal library for money math.

questions.q: What's the difference between `==` and `===`?
options: === checks value AND type without coercion; == coerces types before comparing
questions.explanation: Always prefer ===. == has surprising rules (e.g., '' == 0 is true). === gives predictable behavior.

questions.q: What does the `??` operator do?
options: Nullish coalescing — returns the right side only if the left side is null or undefined
questions.explanation: ?? differs from || by only treating null/undefined as 'missing'. So `0 || 5` is 5, but `0 ?? 5` is 0.

questions.q: What does `user?.address?.city` do?
options: Safely accesses nested properties, returning undefined if any link is null/undefined
Throws an error if user is undefined
Asks the user if they want to access city
questions.explanation: Optional chaining (?.) short-circuits to undefined instead of crashing on null/undefined intermediates.

questions.q: What does the spread operator `...` do in `[...arr1, ...arr2]`?
options: Expands arr1 and arr2 into individual elements of a new array
questions.explanation: Spread is essential for immutable updates and function calls. `Math.max(...nums)` and `[...old, newItem]` are common patterns.

questions.q: What does `2 ** 3` evaluate to?
questions.explanation: ** is the exponentiation operator (ES2016). Equivalent to Math.pow(2, 3).

questions.q: Which is an arrow function?
options: function add(a, b) { return a + b; }
const add = function(a, b) { return a + b; }
questions.explanation: Arrow functions are concise and don't bind their own `this` — making them great for callbacks but unsuitable for object methods that need `this`.

questions.q: What does this return? `function greet(name = 'World') { return 'Hello ' + name; }; greet();`
questions.explanation: Default parameters kick in when an argument is omitted (or explicitly undefined).

questions.q: What is a 'callback function'?
options: A function passed as an argument to another function, to be invoked later
A function that returns to a previous page
questions.explanation: Callbacks underpin JavaScript's async model (setTimeout, event listeners, array methods like .map, .filter).

questions.q: What is a 'pure function'?
options: A function that always returns the same output for the same input and has no side effects
A function written in pure JavaScript
A function declared with the 'pure' keyword
questions.explanation: Pure functions are predictable, testable, and cache-friendly. They form the heart of functional programming.

questions.q: What does `arr.map(x => x * 2)` return?
options: A NEW array with each element doubled
questions.explanation: map() returns a new array of the same length, with the callback's return value at each index. The original is unchanged.

questions.q: What is a 'closure' in JavaScript?
options: A function that 'remembers' variables from the scope where it was defined, even after that scope exits
A way to close a browser tab
A keyword to end a function
questions.explanation: Closures are how counters, private state, and most callback patterns work in JS. A function 'closes over' its surrounding variables.

questions.q: What does this counter pattern demonstrate? `function makeCounter() { let n = 0; return () => ++n; }`
options: A closure capturing private state n
A pure function with no state
questions.explanation: Each call to makeCounter() creates a fresh n. The returned function keeps a private reference to that n via closure.

questions.q: What is 'lexical scope'?
options: Scope is determined by where code is written, not where it's called from
Scope is determined by who calls the function
Scope only applies to global variables
questions.explanation: JavaScript uses lexical (static) scoping. A function's accessible variables are decided by its location in source code.

questions.q: What does the `this` keyword refer to inside a regular function called as `obj.method()`?
options: The object before the dot — obj
questions.explanation: Method invocation: `this` = the object before the dot. Calling the same function standalone makes `this` undefined (strict) or window (sloppy).

questions.q: Why don't arrow functions have their own `this`?
options: They inherit `this` from the enclosing lexical scope, which avoids common bugs in callbacks
They have a hidden this that's hard to access
questions.explanation: Arrow functions' lexical `this` is what makes them perfect for callbacks inside class methods — no more `.bind(this)` boilerplate.

questions.q: Which method REMOVES the last element from an array and returns it?
questions.explanation: pop() = remove last. push() = add last. shift() = remove first. unshift() = add first.

questions.q: What does `[1,2,3,4].filter(x => x > 2)` return?
questions.explanation: filter() returns a new array with only elements where the callback returns truthy.

questions.q: What does `[1,2,3].reduce((sum, n) => sum + n, 0)` return?
questions.explanation: reduce() folds an array into a single value by accumulating. Sum, product, group-by, and many transforms are reduce in disguise.

questions.q: Which method finds the FIRST element matching a condition?
questions.explanation: find() returns the first matching element (or undefined). findIndex() returns its index.

questions.q: What does the spread operator return when combining arrays? `const c = [...a, ...b];`
options: A new array containing all elements of a followed by all elements of b
An array of two arrays: [a, b]
The shorter of the two arrays
questions.explanation: Spread + array literal is the modern, immutable way to concatenate arrays.

questions.q: What does this code log? `const o = {a: 1}; const p = o; p.a = 2; console.log(o.a);`
questions.explanation: Objects are reference types. p and o point to the same object — changing one changes both.

questions.q: Which is the CORRECT way to make a shallow copy of an object?
questions.explanation: Spread (or Object.assign) creates a shallow copy. For deep copies use structuredClone() or a library.

questions.q: What does destructuring do? `const {name, age} = user;`
options: Extracts named properties from user into individual variables
questions.explanation: Destructuring is essential for clean code — function parameters, hook returns, API responses all benefit.

questions.q: What is shorthand property syntax? `const obj = { name };`
options: Equivalent to { name: name } — uses the variable name as both key and value
questions.explanation: If the key and variable have the same name, you can write it once. Saves boilerplate in modern JS code.

questions.q: How do you iterate over an object's KEYS?
questions.explanation: Object.keys(), Object.values(), Object.entries() return arrays you can iterate. Or use `for (const key in obj)`.

questions.q: What is a Promise in JavaScript?
options: An object representing the eventual result (success or failure) of an asynchronous operation
A guarantee from JavaScript to run code
questions.explanation: Promises replace callback hell with a cleaner .then/.catch chain — and underpin async/await.

questions.q: What are the three states of a Promise?
questions.explanation: A pending Promise eventually becomes either fulfilled (with a value) or rejected (with a reason). Settled = either of the latter two.

questions.q: What does .then() return?
questions.explanation: Returning a new Promise is what makes .then chains work. Each .then can return a value or another Promise.

questions.q: How do you handle errors in a Promise chain?
options: Add .catch() at the end of the chain
questions.explanation: .catch() handles any rejection in the chain above it. Always add one — unhandled rejections will eventually crash your app.

questions.q: What does `Promise.all([p1, p2, p3])` do?
options: Returns a Promise that fulfills when ALL inputs fulfill, or rejects on the FIRST rejection
Runs the promises one at a time
questions.explanation: Promise.all is your go-to for parallel async work. Use Promise.allSettled if you want all results regardless of failures.

questions.q: What does the `async` keyword do to a function?
options: Makes it return a Promise automatically, and allows `await` inside it
questions.explanation: An async function always returns a Promise. Even `async () => 1` returns a Promise that resolves to 1.

questions.q: What does `await` do?
options: Pauses the async function until a Promise settles, then resumes with the resolved value
questions.explanation: await only pauses the local async function — the rest of the JS thread keeps running. This is non-blocking concurrency.

questions.q: How do you handle errors with async/await?
options: Wrap the await in a try/catch block
Promise errors don't work with await
questions.explanation: try/catch around await is the standard pattern. Cleaner than .catch chains for sequential logic.

questions.q: What's the MISTAKE in: `async function fetchAll() { const a = await fetch(u1); const b = await fetch(u2); return [a, b]; }`?
options: The fetches run sequentially instead of in parallel — use Promise.all
questions.explanation: Common bug! Each await pauses for the previous. Use `await Promise.all([fetch(u1), fetch(u2)])` for parallel speedup.

questions.q: Can you use `await` at the top level of a module?
options: Yes, in ES modules — top-level await is supported
questions.explanation: Top-level await works in ES modules (since 2020). Useful for dynamic imports and initialization.

questions.q: What is the DOM?
options: A tree-like programming interface representing the structure of an HTML document
questions.explanation: The DOM lets JavaScript read and modify the page structure, styles, and content dynamically.

questions.q: Which method selects the FIRST element matching a CSS selector?
questions.explanation: querySelector is the modern, flexible selector — supports any valid CSS selector. querySelectorAll returns all matches.

questions.q: How do you change an element's text content?
questions.explanation: textContent is safe (no HTML parsing). innerHTML parses HTML but is risky with user input (XSS).

questions.q: How do you attach a click handler to a button?
questions.explanation: addEventListener is the standard. Multiple listeners can be attached for the same event, unlike onClick= assignment.

questions.q: What is 'event bubbling'?
options: When an event fires on a child, it propagates up through parent elements
When events appear in a popup bubble
When events bubble away into nothing
questions.explanation: Bubbling enables event delegation — a single listener on a parent can handle clicks on many children.

questions.q: What does `fetch(url)` return?
options: A Promise that resolves to a Response object
A string of the response body
questions.explanation: fetch resolves to a Response object. You typically chain `.then(r => r.json())` to parse the body.

questions.q: Does fetch reject on HTTP error status (404, 500)?
options: No — it only rejects on network failures. You must check response.ok manually
questions.explanation: A common gotcha. response.ok is true for 200-299. Always check it before assuming success.

questions.q: How do you send a POST request with JSON body using fetch?
questions.explanation: Pass method, headers, and a string body. JSON.stringify() is required — fetch won't auto-serialize objects.

questions.q: What is CORS?
options: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing — a browser security model controlling cross-domain HTTP requests
questions.explanation: CORS errors are one of every web dev's rites of passage. The fix is server-side: send proper Access-Control-Allow-Origin headers.

questions.q: How do you parse a JSON response from fetch?
questions.explanation: Response has helper methods: .json(), .text(), .blob(), .arrayBuffer(). Each returns a Promise.

questions.q: What does template literal syntax look like?
questions.explanation: Backticks + ${} interpolation. Cleaner than concatenation and supports multi-line strings.

questions.q: What does the rest parameter `...args` do in a function signature?
options: Collects all remaining arguments into a real array
questions.explanation: Rest gives you a true array of args, unlike the old `arguments` object.

questions.q: What is a JavaScript module?
options: A file that uses import/export to share code with other files
questions.explanation: ES modules (import/export) are the modern standard. They enable tree-shaking, lazy loading, and proper encapsulation.

questions.q: What's the difference between default and named exports?
options: Default: one per module, imported by any name. Named: many per module, imported by exact name
questions.explanation: Prefer named exports for clarity and refactor safety. Use default for the 'primary' export of a single-purpose module.

questions.q: What does `class Foo extends Bar {}` enable?
options: Foo inherits properties and methods from Bar (prototype-based inheritance with class syntax)
questions.explanation: Classes are syntactic sugar over JS's prototype-based inheritance. Useful for OOP-style code; not always necessary.

questions.q: How do you catch a synchronous error in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: try/catch is the synchronous pattern. For async code, wrap awaits in try/catch or chain .catch().

questions.q: What's the BEST way to handle an error that you can't recover from?
options: Log it and let it propagate with `throw` — don't swallow it silently
questions.explanation: Silent failures are the worst kind. Either handle meaningfully or let it bubble to a higher layer that can.

questions.q: What is `throw new Error('message')` doing?
options: Creating a new Error object and throwing it as an exception
Logging an error to the console
questions.explanation: throw can throw any value, but use Error objects so you get a stack trace and consistent .message/.stack properties.

questions.q: What's a custom error class useful for?
options: Distinguishing different error types so catch blocks can react differently (ValidationError, NotFoundError, etc.)
Making errors look prettier in the console
There's no reason to use them
questions.explanation: Custom error classes let `instanceof` checks branch your error handling — much cleaner than parsing error messages.

questions.q: What is an 'unhandled promise rejection'?
options: A rejected Promise with no .catch handler — modern Node and browsers will warn or crash on these
questions.explanation: Always handle Promise rejections. Unhandled rejections will terminate the process in modern Node.js versions.

questions.q: What does `Boolean('false')` return?
questions.explanation: ANY non-empty string is truthy, including the string 'false'. Only empty string '' is falsy.

questions.q: What does `'5' + 3` evaluate to?
questions.explanation: If either operand is a string, + concatenates. Use Number() or unary + for explicit conversion: `Number('5') + 3 === 8`.

questions.q: What does `'5' - 3` evaluate to?
questions.explanation: Unlike +, the - operator has no string meaning, so it coerces operands to numbers. JS is wild.

questions.q: What does `[] == false` evaluate to?
options: true (== coerces both to numbers, both become 0)
questions.explanation: Another == oddity. Stick to === to avoid these traps in production code.

questions.q: What's a SAFE way to check if a variable is null or undefined?
options: if (value == null)  — intentionally uses == to match both null and undefined
questions.explanation: value == null is the one accepted use of == in many style guides — it cleanly catches both null and undefined.

questions.q: What does `localStorage.setItem('key', 'value')` do?
options: Persists a key/value string in the browser, surviving page reloads
questions.explanation: localStorage is synchronous, ~5MB limit, strings only. For objects, JSON.stringify before storing.

questions.q: What's the difference between localStorage and sessionStorage?
options: localStorage persists until cleared; sessionStorage is wiped when the tab closes
questions.explanation: Pick based on lifetime needs. sessionStorage is great for in-progress form data; localStorage for user preferences.

questions.q: What does `setTimeout(fn, 1000)` do?
options: Schedules fn to run AFTER at least 1000ms (non-blocking)
Adds 1000ms to the page load time
questions.explanation: JS is single-threaded but non-blocking. setTimeout queues fn for later; the rest of your code keeps running immediately.

questions.q: What is the Console API mostly used for?
questions.explanation: console has many useful methods beyond log: table, group, time/timeEnd, assert, trace. Worth exploring.

questions.q: What does `window.location.href = 'https://example.com'` do?
options: Navigates the browser to that URL
Fetches the page in the background
questions.explanation: Assigning to location.href performs a full navigation. For SPA-style routing without reload, use the History API or a router.

questions.q: What does `JSON.stringify(obj)` do?
options: Converts a JavaScript object/array to a JSON string
Parses a JSON string into an object
questions.explanation: Essential for storage and transport. Skips functions, undefined, and Symbol values. Pass `null, 2` for pretty-printing.

questions.q: What does `JSON.parse(str)` do?
options: Converts a JSON string into a JavaScript value
questions.explanation: Inverse of stringify. Throws SyntaxError on malformed JSON — always wrap in try/catch for untrusted input.

questions.q: Why might `JSON.stringify({a: undefined, b: 1})` surprise you?
options: The undefined value is omitted from the output: '{"b":1}'
questions.explanation: JSON has no undefined. Functions and undefined values silently disappear when stringifying objects.

questions.q: What's the safe way to deep-clone a JSON-safe object?
questions.explanation: structuredClone() (since 2022) is the modern built-in deep clone. JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(obj)) is the old workaround.

questions.q: Can a JSON file contain comments?
options: No — pure JSON does not support comments
questions.explanation: Common pain point. Use JSONC or JSON5 if you need comments (but they're not standard JSON).

questions.q: Which loop iterates over the VALUES of an array?
questions.explanation: for...of is for iterables (arrays, strings, Maps, Sets). for...in is for object keys — don't use it on arrays!

questions.q: Why is `for...in` BAD for iterating arrays?
options: It iterates keys as strings and includes inherited properties — order is also not guaranteed
It only works in old browsers
questions.explanation: Use for...of, .forEach(), or a classic for loop with indices for arrays. for...in is for plain objects only.

questions.q: What does `.forEach` NOT support that a for loop does?
options: break and continue — forEach can't be exited early
questions.explanation: forEach always runs to completion. Use for...of (with break) or .some()/.every() if you need early exit.

questions.q: What does `Array.from({length: 5}, (_, i) => i)` produce?
questions.explanation: Common pattern for generating sequences. Array.from accepts an array-like + a map function.

questions.q: How do you iterate over an object's [key, value] PAIRS?
options: for (const k in obj) — only gets keys
questions.explanation: Object.entries + destructuring is the cleanest way. Or Object.keys + .map((k) => [k, obj[k]]).

questions.q: Which keyword should you AVOID in modern JavaScript?
questions.explanation: var has function scope and hoisting quirks. Modern code uses const by default, let when reassignment is needed.

questions.q: What does 'use strict' do at the top of a file?
options: Enables stricter parsing and error handling (e.g., assigning to undeclared variables throws)
questions.explanation: Strict mode catches more mistakes. ES modules are strict by default — explicit 'use strict' is mostly legacy now.

questions.q: Why is mutating function arguments BAD?
options: It causes surprising side effects for the caller and breaks the principle of pure functions
It is forbidden in strict mode
questions.explanation: Return new values instead of mutating inputs. Predictability is worth a tiny performance overhead.

questions.q: What is the 'principle of least surprise' in JS?
options: Code should behave the way readers would intuitively expect — favor clarity over cleverness
Hide as much logic as possible
questions.explanation: Boring code is good code. Reach for clever tricks only when they meaningfully improve clarity or performance.

questions.q: Which is a HEALTHY rule of thumb for modern JS?
options: Prefer const, use === over ==, prefer immutability, handle Promise rejections, type your data (TS)
questions.explanation: These habits prevent the most common production bugs. TypeScript on top adds another safety net.

questions.q: What is npm?
options: Node Package Manager — installs and manages JavaScript libraries
questions.explanation: npm is the default package manager bundled with Node.js. Alternatives: pnpm (faster, disk-efficient) and yarn.

questions.q: What's the purpose of `package.json`?
options: Lists a project's dependencies, scripts, metadata, and other config
questions.explanation: Every Node/npm project has one. Run `npm init` to create. `dependencies` for runtime libs, `devDependencies` for build/test tools.

questions.q: What is a 'bundler' like Vite or Webpack?
options: A tool that combines, transforms, and optimizes your source files for production
questions.explanation: Bundlers handle modules, JSX/TS compilation, code splitting, minification, dev servers — they make modern frontend possible.

questions.q: What is TypeScript?
options: A superset of JavaScript that adds static types, compiled to JavaScript before running
questions.explanation: TS catches bugs at compile time that JS would only catch at runtime. Now the default for serious frontend projects.

questions.q: What does ESLint do?
options: Statically analyzes JS/TS code to flag stylistic and potential bug issues
questions.explanation: Linters keep code consistent across teams and catch many subtle bugs. Pair with Prettier for autoformatting.

questions.q: What's the FIRST thing to do in a new JS project?
options: Run `npm init` (or scaffold via Vite/CRA/Next), commit to git, write a README
Start coding immediately with no setup
questions.explanation: Proper setup pays compound interest. Even a 1-hour project benefits from git + a README + a tiny package.json.

questions.q: Why are AUTOMATED TESTS valuable in JS projects?
options: They catch regressions when you change code, enable safe refactoring, and document expected behavior
They are only for big companies
They replace the need for understanding the code
questions.explanation: Tests are an investment in your future self's velocity. Start with the most critical paths; expand from there.

questions.q: What's the value of a `.gitignore` file in a JS project?
options: Excludes node_modules, build artifacts, secrets, and .env files from version control
questions.explanation: Without it you'd commit 100MB+ of node_modules and possibly leak secrets. ALWAYS configure .gitignore early.

questions.q: When debugging in the browser, which tool is MOST powerful?
options: DevTools' debugger with breakpoints, watch expressions, and the call stack
questions.explanation: console.log is great; the debugger is greater. Learning breakpoints + step-through is a career-multiplying skill.

questions.q: What's the best way to keep IMPROVING at JavaScript?
options: Build real projects, read good code, write tests, learn the standard library deeply, follow MDN
questions.explanation: Real practice + reading great code + the official MDN docs is the proven recipe. Compound learning beats binge learning.
